item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is pretty cool. I really like the effort that went into it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needles, Dagget, Barstow, Yermo, Fort Irwin (NTC)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Correct! I had trouble finding somewhere in Death Valley that wasn't a well-known portion of a national park, so I made something up.

On the upside, Saint Amaro is pretty nifty.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: As far as I can determine, there's no such place as San Amaro Valley California

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mmm, fictitious genetics used in a scientifically valid fashion.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay, thanks everyone!

MrWrong: Yeah, the ivory was there, but just barely. I have a bad tendency to try and be a vague as possible on the first go, which is why I especially appreciate feedback.

canaryfarmer: Yeah, the black dots were totally a last-minute ploy because I was sick of trying to figure out how to balance various layers. I've gone back and tweaked it a bit.

Edit: Sergeimosin: I'll think on it, but it's finals week and I have to defend my thesis in two weeks, so it'll be a bit.

As far as the O5s go, I see the logic like this:

+ show block
– hide block

We definitely know that there are ants that can survive tunneling through magma. We can assume from the debris that there's at least one other species involved, and what appear to be artifacts of relatively modern material science and electronics. The genetic abilities/contents are sort of circumstantial evidence beyond that.

If we assume that an intelligent species created the ants, then it's not unreasonable to assume that said species, without any knowledge of their ultimate motivations or views on humanity, is 1) interested enough in the surface to check it out and 2) technologically/biologically capable of sending larger (potentially megafauna-sized) stuff that way, and 3) based on the return rate, probably not in a huge hurry. I figure the O5 Council, by a close vote, are erring on the side of caution by acting on that string of assumptions and getting the ball rolling on a very long-term project, rather than doing nothing. I figure the dissenters either thought it wasn't appropriate to establish communications or wanted more evidence of another species existing, although I don't really see the Council as being gunshy.

Agent MacLeod and others: Now now, everybody. Moles are Talpidae (among others), crabs are Brachyura, and people are Hominidae.

These guys are something like…
http://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/9/9e/Humpback_stellwagen_edit.jpg
+
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Giant_pangolin#/media/File:PholidotusAfricanusWolf.jpg
+
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Turbinellidae#/media/File:Columbarium_wormaldi.JPG

Edit 2: Also, as for these dudes:
Edit 2: Also, as for these dudes:

In my head I don't see them so much as spying, as just having "recently" figured out that there is a surface. I figure that genetic code coming back also has a lot of sections like, "There are these winged feathery things that eat us!" and "There are these scaly green things that eat us!" in there with all the "The tall ones in the white coats keep stealing our queens!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is super cool. Ants are weird enough, and every bit of this article made things weirder along the way. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Crab people, crab people

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Good job here! I didn't mind the extended delay regarding the truly anomalous nature, as I figured it was coming, what with a Level 4 Access collapsible plainly visible at the bottom.

My only issue is with the photo—the black dots were obviously added in later and they look super-fake (actually, to clarify, they look fine in the small size as presented on the page, but look like crisp pixels in the larger version it links to. Keeping the photo at the smaller size should work.)

The info-graphic, btw, is much appreciated and well-done.

+1!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eusocial insects often seem like an anomaly themselves. I liked this. Great use of genetic details. Upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Very well written, and I appreciate the inclusion of the infographic, it adds a nice touch
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have to agree with piefish here. I think you could really improve this SCP by introducing the concept of the mole people a little earlier. It really does feel like an SCP about slow breeding ants that tunnel downward until the very end of the article.

Also, perhaps consider adding some additional support for the hypothesis that the ants are spying (maybe some kind of detectable pheromone signal?), because at the moment, it feels like the O5s are just kind of jumping to a conclusion with very limited evidence to support it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

Very interesting SCP, an all together well made Euclid class SCP. The idea is new and fresh, and the extra documents at the end put the icing on the cake.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fantastic work. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yay, thanks! As for erosion…

}.}… {.{… a wizard did it. Yeah, I've got nothing :(
Would it help if I blackboxed the 1914 date?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, admittedly they don't come in until the addenda. I thought about working in other odd angles, like how they can survive tunneling through magma, but it kept getting excessively specific and kind of silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When it was but a draft, I have second feelings as to what it leads to in the end. And now, the reveal kinda hit me head-on (granted, I didn't recall ivory when I read it or missed it entirely).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, new one up! Thanks to MrWrong and Psygent for feedback on this. I'm kind of pulling the trigger on this now, because I need to stop fiddling around with it in my sandbox and work on my thesis.

Image credits: Original anthill photo was taken by Jim Kennedy under CC by 2.0, and is available here: https://www.flickr.com/photos/nature80020/14969387821/

It was overlain with an image of the east face painted hills of Oregon, licensed CC by-sa 3.0 by wikimedia user Finetooth and available here: http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:East_face_painted_hills_panorama.jpg

The chart started life as a US Geological Survey chart of strata in Bryce canyon, and is under public domain, available here: http://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Bryce_strat.jpg#filelinks

Also, the broad idea behind SCP-XXXX-3 is an homage to a science fiction story I read some years ago about a strange subterrean geometric/metallic race and humanity discovering eachother tangentially, eventually resulting in the subterranean race killing all surface life by trying to visit. The races are otherwise not similar.

Edit: Now the inspiration of a concept album by Thetataurus, available here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really want to upvote this. It's written perfectly, it's interesting and the idea of ants being used as bio engineered reconnaissance thing is awesome. I think my issue is that it took too long to get to what I just mentioned (unless I'm just dumb and missed it). I spent far too long thinking "This is a very well written piece about ants that are a bit odd" rather than "holy balls the mole people are spying on us with ants"

This is in no way bad though. I do like it it's just the above is holding me back from upvoting. neutral vote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Has the colony that produced the sample debris field been in Foundation containment for hundreds of years? Otherwise the expectation that erosion and other natural forces would not have obscured or disturbed the pattern is unrealistic.

But aside from that minor suspension of disbelief this is an incredible entry I really love it!
